professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: For this week, we have been exploring how programming is taught now that AI coding assistants are everywhere. Some instructors argue that students should first master writing code by hand, so they truly grasp the underlying logic, while others believe that learning to direct these tools is the real modern skill. Should introductory courses require students to code without AI help? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think beginners need to struggle through the basics themselves before leaning on any assistant. When I let autocomplete write my early exercises, I could not debug anything later, because I never understood why the code actually worked. That foundation only comes from doing it manually.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Forcing people to ignore tools that professionals rely on daily feels backward. The genuinely important skill is judging whether generated code is correct and then adapting it, and students can build real understanding by carefully reading and fixing whatever the assistant produces.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: Over the past several weeks, we've examined how museums acquired many of their most celebrated collections. Some historians argue that artifacts taken during colonial rule should be returned to their countries of origin, while others insist that major museums preserve and share these objects with a truly global audience. Should museums be required to return such artifacts? Why or why not?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I believe objects taken without consent belong back home, where they still carry cultural and spiritual meaning for the communities that created them. A display case in a distant city strips that context away entirely. Returning them is a matter of basic respect, not merely legal ownership.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: In my opinion, large museums actually protect these pieces and let millions of people encounter cultures they would otherwise never see. Many origin countries lack the funding or stable conditions to conserve fragile artifacts, so a blanket return policy could put them at genuine risk.
